seo.title: Google ADK Guardrails for Cybersecurity
seo.description: Compliance guide for Google ADK agents in cybersecurity. Meet NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Cybersecurity teams using Google ADK to build multi-agent systems for threat detection and response face heightened risks due to the potential for AI-driven errors like circular agent dependencies or inconsistent model outputs to leak sensitive threat intelligence or trigger erroneous automated responses. Compliance guardrails are essential to align with NIST CSF, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 while preventing adversary exploitation of leaked data or unverified AI advisories. Vex provides pre-agent callbacks to enforce reliability and regulatory adherence in these critical environments.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires robust access controls and data classification to prevent unauthorized exposure of threat intelligence via LLM APIs.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex's before_agent_callback scans agent inputs/outputs for PII, classified data leaks, and enforces data masking before Gemini model calls.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates monitoring of automated systems to prevent false positives causing service outages in incident response.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex detects multi-agent circular dependencies and session state corruption, blocking faulty chains that could trigger erroneous alerts.

complianceRequirements.regulation: ISO 27001 - A.12.6.1 Management of Technical Vulnerabilities
complianceRequirements.description: Requires verification of AI-generated security advisories against authoritative sources like CVE databases.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex validates advisory outputs against CVE APIs and blocks unverified threat intel from propagating across agents.

complianceRequirements.description: Demands human approval gates for automated response actions in critical systems.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Vex intercepts high-risk tool calls (e.g., firewall changes) and enforces human-in-loop approval workflows.

industryRisks.name: Threat Intelligence Leakage
industryRisks.description: Gemini model APIs inadvertently expose IOCs or TTPs to external providers during multi-agent processing.
industryRisks.example: Agent delegates malware sample analysis to Gemini, leaking hash values that adversaries scrape from API logs.

industryRisks.name: False Positive Incident Escalation
industryRisks.description: Circular agent dependencies create feedback loops amplifying benign anomalies into critical alerts.
industryRisks.example: Threat hunter agent flags log anomaly → response agent blocks IP → monitoring agent detects 'outage' → escalation loop.

industryRisks.name: Unverified CVE Advisory Propagation
industryRisks.description: AI-generated advisories spread across agents without CVE database validation, confusing SOC analysts.
industryRisks.example: Vulnerability scanner agent fabricates CVE-2024-XXXX advisory that cascades to patch management agent.

industryRisks.name: Unauthorized Automated Response
industryRisks.description: Session state corruption causes agents to execute critical actions without human approval.
industryRisks.example: Corrupted conversation state triggers firewall quarantine of legitimate C2 server during red team exercise.

implementation.title: Install Vex Guardrails for Google ADK
implementation.description: Add Vex as before_agent_callback to catch data leaks and circular dependencies before agent execution.

implementation.title: Human Approval for Critical Actions
implementation.description: Intercept high-risk tool calls like network quarantine or endpoint isolation.

implementation.title: Session State Validation
implementation.description: Prevent corruption in long-running threat hunting conversations.

caseStudyScenario.heading: Google ADK in Cybersecurity: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario: A global MSSP deploys Google ADK multi-agent system for automated threat hunting across 500+ customer environments. The ThreatHunter agent (Gemini 1.5 Pro) processes EDR telemetry, delegates to VulnerabilityScanner agent for CVE correlation, and passes findings to IncidentResponder for automated containment. During a nation-state APT simulation, the system detects anomalous C2 traffic and begins IOC extraction across agents. However, circular delegation occurs when VulnerabilityScanner calls ThreatHunter for context, creating an infinite loop that leaks customer IOCs to Gemini APIs. Without guardrails, the IncidentResponder executes IP blocks on legitimate geo-blocking services, causing customer outages while unverified CVE advisories flood analyst queues.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: Vex guardrails activate immediately: before_agent_callback blocks the circular dependency after 5 hops, masks IOCs before Gemini calls, and requires human approval for containment actions. The CVE validation policy flags fabricated advisories, preventing SOC alert fatigue. Human analysts review the sanitized context, confirm the red team exercise, and safely terminate the simulation. Zero data leakage occurs, no customer impact, and the incident is closed in 15 minutes versus 4+ hours without Vex.

cta.heading: Comply with Cybersecurity Regulations Using Vex
cta.description: Add Vex guardrails to your Google ADK deployment today to prevent threat intel leaks and ensure NIST/SOC 2 compliance.
